full_transcript:
SPEAKER_01: "So we've established that there's this strong connection between emotions and memories."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, quite a bit of research has gone into investigating the connection."
SPEAKER_01: "One study, conducted by a research team at Harvard, looked at how emotions affect how much we remember, how many details of a memory we're able to remember."
SPEAKER_01: "In the study, volunteers were shown a series of pictures."
SPEAKER_01: "Some were positive images, like a child smiling, some were negative, and some were just neutral."
SPEAKER_01: "Can anyone guess which images were the easiest to remember?"
SPEAKER_00: "The most positive and negative images?"
SPEAKER_00: "The ones that triggered the strongest emotional responses?"
SPEAKER_01: "Right."
SPEAKER_01: "Those images that generated a more extreme emotional response, positive or negative, they were easier to recall than the neutral images."
SPEAKER_01: "Pretty intuitive, right?"

SPEAKER_01: "But, the scientists also found that people remember more details from a negative image than they do from a positive one."
SPEAKER_01: "How come?"
SPEAKER_01: "Well, we think it's rooted in how we respond to highly stressful or dangerous situations."
SPEAKER_01: "By remembering a negative event in detail, well, those details might be really helpful if someday you're faced with a similar situation."
SPEAKER_00: "I think I know what you mean."
SPEAKER_00: "When I was younger I was climbing a ladder and one of the rungs was wet and I nearly slipped and fell."
SPEAKER_00: "I remember just how scared I felt and I can still see the little beads of water on that step."
SPEAKER_01: "And I bet you're careful around matters to this day, aren't you?"
SPEAKER_01: "Now, another question that researchers have focused on is, are there specific areas of the brain associated with different emotional states?"
SPEAKER_01: "One neuroscientist, Antonio Demasio, tested this idea."
SPEAKER_01: "Demasio hypothesized that different emotions would trigger activity in different parts of the brain."

SPEAKER_01: "To test his hypothesis, a team of researchers hooked up some volunteers to brain scanning equipment and asked them to recall, to relive, actually, some intense memories, like the elation they felt on graduation day."
SPEAKER_01: "and the results supported Demasio's hypothesis."
SPEAKER_01: "Remembering a happy event did indeed activate one area of the brain while remembering a sad event activated another."
SPEAKER_01: "This finding that emotions of a specific type are associated with a specific brain region may help explain why, when you're feeling a certain way, the brain will activate memories of past situations associated with that particular feeling."
SPEAKER_01: "A phenomenon we call mood congruity."
SPEAKER_01: "Congruity means something like harmony between two things."
SPEAKER_01: "So mood congruity works like this."
SPEAKER_01: "If you're feeling happy, it's more likely that your brain will call up pleasant memories, like that wonderful beach vacation you took last year, which of course just reinforces your happy mood."
SPEAKER_01: "The brain is wired to support your mood."
SPEAKER_01: "It's not going to try to balance or temper it by producing memories that aren't consistent with the way you're feeling at that moment."

SPEAKER_00: "And some design reformers worried that the nation could lose its advantage in international trade if designs weren't changed."
SPEAKER_00: "So the new technical abilities in production introduced by the Industrial Revolution prompted or enabled these new wallpaper designs."

SPEAKER_00: "You see, manufacturers had figured out how to use engraved metal rollers in the production of wallpaper."
SPEAKER_00: "Using metal rollers to print wallpaper allowed a level of extremely fine detail and shading that previously had not been possible."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, before, the papers were being made by block printing, which was pretty imprecise."
SPEAKER_00: "Plus, the new production techniques made wallpaper a lot cheaper to buy, since it reduced production costs."
SPEAKER_00: "And these new wallpapers were popular."
SPEAKER_00: "To the average industrialist, good design meant whatever sold the best."
SPEAKER_00: "Popular taste equated ornate embellishment with excellence, so many manufacturers created wallpapers with as much surface decoration as possible."
SPEAKER_00: "Graphic elements from different eras were combined without regard to period or stylistic unity, and designs became increasingly cluttered, all of which irritated design purists."
SPEAKER_00: "Even the Victorian-era writers mocked the wallpaper design trend."
SPEAKER_00: "In his 1852 short story, A House Full of Horrors, the writer Henry Morley described the garish combination of mismatched design elements that represented the new style in wallpaper design."
SPEAKER_00: "Quote, the paper in my parlor contains four kinds of birds of paradise besides bridges and pagodas."
SPEAKER_00: "Wilkie Collins described another of the new wallpapers as, quote, a gaudy pattern of birds, trellis work, and flowers in gold, red, and green on a white background, end quote."
SPEAKER_00: "One technique in particular became associated with the new wallpapers, Trump-Loy."
SPEAKER_00: "Trump-Loy, that's French for tricking or cheating the eye, was a design technique that created visual illusions."
SPEAKER_00: "So you'd have wallpaper that actually looked like marble or wood grain or maybe like aged leather."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, the design reform critics, they opposed this."

SPEAKER_00: "They thought that Trump-Loy designs were inherently deceptive because they made walls look like something they were not."
SPEAKER_00: "This was also, this was the design reformers critique of the three-dimensional illusions of depth."
SPEAKER_00: "To them, it was all a type of lying."
SPEAKER_00: "So these designs were actually immoral."
SPEAKER_00: "And according to their shaky logic, if people filled their houses with wallpapers that were visually deceitful, then the inhabitants would become deceitful too."
SPEAKER_00: "Yeah, right."
SPEAKER_00: "When they created wallpapers, design reform advocates avoided illusions and visual deceptions."
SPEAKER_00: "They created primarily geometric two-dimensional patterns, which they said would confirm the wall as a flat surface rather than disguise or misrepresent it."
SPEAKER_00: "And they used fewer and more subdued colors in their wallpapers."
SPEAKER_00: "So it's probably not surprising that design reform products didn't do well in the marketplace."
SPEAKER_00: "From the 1840s on, the three-dimensional and trompe-loy designs continued to sell in huge quantities, despite their supposed corrupting influence."
SPEAKER_00: "Meanwhile, design reforms geometric patterns sold in small quantities and were used mainly to decorate government buildings instead of homes."

SPEAKER_00: "So, we've talked about the plates that form the Earth's crust and their movements and how in some places they're separating."

SPEAKER_00: "This is a lot like a geyser except it's on the ocean floor."
SPEAKER_00: "A geyser, of course, is a kind of eruption from an underground hot spring."
SPEAKER_00: "The water that's been heated up in the Earth's interior, when under pressure, can erupt, sending that water and steam shooting upwards through a crack in the Earth."
SPEAKER_00: "A hydrothermal vent is essentially the same thing, but the water is emitted out of cracks or fractures in the ocean floor."
SPEAKER_00: "If you've heard about hydrothermal vents at all, it's probably because of the exotic life forms around them."
SPEAKER_00: "Tube worms, giant clams, that kind of thing."
SPEAKER_00: "Forms that don't depend on energy from the sun, but depend on chemical energy."

SPEAKER_00: "But the vents are also of enormous significance for us from a purely geological perspective, because the chemistry of the oceans is affected by them."
SPEAKER_00: "To see how, let's look at the process a little more closely."
SPEAKER_00: "They typically occur in fields, so you might have an area with a dozen of them."
SPEAKER_00: "But you need two things to get one of these fields."
SPEAKER_00: "First, you gotta have heat."
SPEAKER_00: "And you've got to have fishers in the ocean floor."
SPEAKER_00: "So in a vent field, you've got cracks in the ocean floor and cold water at the bottom of the ocean."
SPEAKER_00: "We're talking maybe two degrees Celsius goes down into them."
SPEAKER_00: "As it goes underground it heats up because in these fields there are magma chambers only a few kilometers below the ocean floor."
SPEAKER_00: "This hot molten rock heats the solid rock above it to as high as 500 degrees Celsius and this heated solid rock then heats the ocean water that flows over it."

SPEAKER_00: "Now remember, the high pressure of the deep sea allows water to stay liquid at such a high temperature, so it can reach temperatures of 3 or 400 degrees Celsius."
SPEAKER_00: "As the water is heated, it rises up through other cracks and it shoots up back into the ocean, much like with geysers on land."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, the important part is what the water is carrying with it as it emerges."
SPEAKER_00: "The heated water draws minerals from solid rock, so you've got dissolved metals in the water, like iron and copper."
SPEAKER_00: "When the water shoots up and re-enters the cold ocean, it quickly cools and these minerals precipitate out."
SPEAKER_00: "They're released and they're deposited into the ocean water, which affects its composition."
SPEAKER_00: "And it also creates quite a sight."
SPEAKER_00: "These vents have a plume that looks like smoke, like smoke that's coming up out of the vent in the earth."
SPEAKER_00: "Remember, some of the water coming out of the vents is over 300 degrees Celsius."

SPEAKER_01: "One reason is that it's quite easy to observe."
SPEAKER_01: "It lives out in the open on mud flats and has a very limited territory."
SPEAKER_01: "In fact, it rarely leaves this mud environment."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, the Fiddler Crab has an unusual way of seeing."
SPEAKER_01: "As you'll notice from this picture, its eyes are located on stalks that point straight up."
SPEAKER_01: "This gives the crab a panoramic visual field."
SPEAKER_01: "Here, let me show you what I mean."
SPEAKER_01: "So the fiddler crab can see 360 degrees, all around in every direction."
SPEAKER_01: "And the end of each stalk is divided into about 10,000 little eyes."
SPEAKER_01: "So it has compound vision, similar to what some insects have."

SPEAKER_01: "Another unusual feature is that this crab's vision is sharpest on the periphery on the outer edges of its visual field."
SPEAKER_01: "Actually, this makes a lot more sense when you realize that it's on the edge of its visual field on the ground where the other crabs are likely to be, that they have to make the finest distinctions like between rival and mate."
SPEAKER_01: "But even the sharpest part of the crab's visual field isn't all that great."
SPEAKER_01: "It'd be like, oh, imagine looking at a newspaper and say you saw nothing but a blurry white object, maybe with a few black spots on it."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay?"
SPEAKER_01: "Now, another important thing animals have to be able to see is predators."
SPEAKER_01: "For fiddler crabs, the most important predators are typically birds."
SPEAKER_01: "And the very center of the fiddler crab's field of vision is, surprise, the sky."
SPEAKER_01: "And here's the thing."
SPEAKER_01: "The crab's visual field is even blurrier in the center, up in the sky, than it is on the edges."

SPEAKER_00: "In fact, let's look, yes, David."
SPEAKER_01: "Sorry, but I was just wondering about photography."
SPEAKER_01: "I'm guessing that photographs can show a greater range, a greater dynamic range than paintings, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "I'm glad you asked that."

SPEAKER_00: "But first, to answer your question,"
SPEAKER_00: "Surprisingly, the dynamic range in a lot of traditional photography isn't much greater because of the film and paper that's used."

SPEAKER_00: "Take Ansel Adams."
SPEAKER_00: "I know you've all seen his photographs."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, he developed a system in 1941, he called it the zone system."
SPEAKER_00: "Imagine a scale from pure black to pure white."
SPEAKER_00: "Atoms divided that scale, the whole range of light intensities into 10 equal zones."

SPEAKER_02: "Well, why don't we take a closer look at these auroras?"
SPEAKER_02: "All right."

SPEAKER_02: "The sun's matter just sort of seething all the time."
SPEAKER_02: "The explosions from the sun, they blow tons of super hot, electrically charged particles out into space."

SPEAKER_02: "Right."

SPEAKER_02: "Okay."

SPEAKER_02: "Earth's magnetic field deflects most of the solar wind, makes it flow around Earth without getting very close."
SPEAKER_02: "And that's actually quite important because these high energy particles could be dangerous for living organisms like us if they ever reached Earth's surface."

SPEAKER_01: "Very dry grass is almost like tinder, and it will burn very easily and very quickly."

SPEAKER_01: "What comes to mind?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, don't they live in igloos and houses made of snow?"

SPEAKER_01: "Actually, that was true for some Inuit groups, but not for the group we're talking about near the Mackenzie River."
SPEAKER_01: "And we're talking about traditional Inuit homes before the influence of Europeans."
SPEAKER_01: "Today, they live in houses more like yours and mine."
SPEAKER_01: "So this group of Inuit near the Mackenzie River spent most of the winter in permanent houses, which were made out of wood."

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_02: "Okay, here's an interesting word, astrobiology."
SPEAKER_02: "Have you come across the term astrobiology before?"
SPEAKER_01: "It sounds like a cross between biology and astronomy."
SPEAKER_01: "Yes."
SPEAKER_01: "So the study of life, astro, stars, space, so something to do with studying life in space"
SPEAKER_02: "Yes, precisely."
SPEAKER_02: "It's a relatively new science that was on the margins for a while, but it has now reached respectability."
SPEAKER_02: "It has aspects of biology, astronomy, and geology, but maybe it's also philosophy?"
SPEAKER_02: "I mean, we're dealing with the unknown, making assumptions, speculations, which, well, some think this goes a little beyond the usual role of science."
SPEAKER_02: "But anyway, one thing we're doing in astrobiology is that we're looking very carefully at microbes."
SPEAKER_02: "Bacteria are one example of microbes."
SPEAKER_02: "They're tiny organisms."

SPEAKER_02: "You need a microscope to see them."
SPEAKER_02: "So astrobiologists are interested in whether microbes might have been carried to Earth on rocks from other planets."
SPEAKER_02: "And studies of meteorite found in Antarctica seem to suggest that possibility."
SPEAKER_02: "But it's not just microbes from other planets traveling to Earth that scientists are studying."
SPEAKER_02: "Going in the other direction, they're looking at whether microbes from Earth might have been carried to other planets."
SPEAKER_00: "So could life that traveled from here exist somewhere else?"
SPEAKER_02: "Yes, that's what's fascinating about this."
SPEAKER_02: "But first, well, we better back up a bit."
SPEAKER_02: "How could this happen, microbes going from one planet to another?"
SPEAKER_02: "One possibility is from the impact of asteroids and comets on planets."
SPEAKER_02: "When these objects hit a planet's surface, they form a crater, a hole, and material, well, rocks, get ejected from that crater and thrown up into the planet's atmosphere."
SPEAKER_02: "Some of the rocks might even escape the planet's gravity and go into outer space."
SPEAKER_02: "Okay, most of the life in the ejected rocks would die due to the extremely high pressures and the high speed of the rocks as they're being thrown into space."

SPEAKER_02: "But if the living organism was inside a rock on the surface of the planet, but not right where the asteroid hit, there wouldn't be as much pressure on the rock and the organism might survive when the rock gets ejected into space."
SPEAKER_02: "Then there's the question of survival in space."
SPEAKER_02: "Uh, yes?"
SPEAKER_00: "I don't see how that could happen."
SPEAKER_00: "I thought anything living would die in outer space."
SPEAKER_02: "There's no air, no food."
SPEAKER_02: "Yes, and there's extreme temperatures."
SPEAKER_02: "Cosmic rays, ultraviolet radiation."
SPEAKER_02: "But experiments involving bacteria in space have shown that they can survive these conditions."
SPEAKER_02: "If a microbe is deep enough inside a rock, it'll be protected from ultraviolet light, which is particularly deadly."

SPEAKER_00: "Then as springtime approaches, the starch is converted back into sugar."
SPEAKER_00: "The sugars and starch is dissolved in this watery liquid that we call sap, and they're moved around to various places in the tree in vessels that are referred to as phloem."

SPEAKER_00: "So, phloem can either move sugar down toward the roots or up toward the leaves or horizontally from one side of the tree to another."
SPEAKER_00: "During the spring in proper weather conditions, the sap in the tree begins to rise."
SPEAKER_00: "And for this sap to move throughout the tree, the weather conditions have to be just right when the days become mild above freezing and the nights are still freezing."
SPEAKER_00: "So there needs to be an alternate warm day and below freezing night."
SPEAKER_00: "We're still not totally sure exactly how this promotes sap movement."
SPEAKER_00: "The most popular theory involves the unique structures found in maple trees."
SPEAKER_00: "These phloem vessels are surrounded by other cells, which in most species of trees are filled with fluid."
SPEAKER_00: "But in the maple trees, these are mostly filled with gas."
SPEAKER_00: "During the night when it's cold, the gas in these cells contracts, causing the cells to shrink."
SPEAKER_00: "This creates a low pressure in the tree, so the higher pressure in the roots pushes the sap up."
SPEAKER_00: "So during the night the sap moves up into the trunk and the leaves, okay?"

SPEAKER_00: "The pressure is lower in the tree than it is in the roots."
SPEAKER_00: "Then, during the day, as the temperature rises, the gases in these cells expand."
SPEAKER_00: "The cells heat up, so they expand, creating greater pressure in the tree."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the pressure is higher in the tree, moving the sap in various directions."
SPEAKER_00: "The important thing for collecting the sap is since there's this pressure in the tree, if you put a hole into the tree, it's called tapping."
SPEAKER_00: "The sap will flow out of the tree due to this pressure."
SPEAKER_00: "We're often asked, you know, doesn't that hurt the trees when you drill holes in them?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, the sap you extract is only a small portion of the tree's total production, and the hole, it heals over by the next year."
SPEAKER_00: "So a maple tree does need to be about 40 years old before it can be tapped, but a good tree, if taken care of properly, can produce sap year after year for well over 100 years."
SPEAKER_00: "So anyway, there are several species of maples."
SPEAKER_00: "The most common species that's used is the sugar maple, followed by the black maple, are the most common types tapped."

SPEAKER_02: "We're going to talk today about a couple of experiments related to learning in infants or babies."
SPEAKER_02: "Those areas are memory and imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "Memory and imitation, so you read Chapter 8 of your textbook, right?"
SPEAKER_02: "It deals with these topics."
SPEAKER_02: "So I'll have some questions for you."
SPEAKER_02: "The two studies I want to look at, they've been controversial."
SPEAKER_02: "That's because they show these things happening before or earlier than we used to think."
SPEAKER_02: "So first, let's look at an experiment by Caroline Roeve Collier."
SPEAKER_02: "She published an important study that looked at the cognitive abilities of infants."
SPEAKER_02: "What can you tell me about Roeve Collier's study?"
SPEAKER_01: "Um, she had a baby in a crib and above the baby there was a toy, a mobile hanging above the crib."
SPEAKER_01: "And a ribbon was tied from the mobile to the baby's leg."
SPEAKER_01: "Um, so when the baby kicked the mobile moved."
SPEAKER_01: "There's a picture in your book."
SPEAKER_01: "So yeah, when they returned the baby to the crib weeks later, the baby kicked its leg when it saw the mobile again."
SPEAKER_01: "Its foot wasn't tied to the mobile, but it was still kicking."

SPEAKER_00: "But Roe v. Collier also went on to say that was proof, evidence, that the baby had remembered something, had a memory."
SPEAKER_00: "The baby had kept that information that kicking moves the mobile."
SPEAKER_02: "Good."
SPEAKER_02: "In those experiments, she typically had a two-and-a-half-month-old infant."
SPEAKER_02: "The baby kicks and gets to see the mobile move."
SPEAKER_02: "So Roevee Collier concludes that very young infants can remember retain information like this."
SPEAKER_02: "So what did critics say about her conclusions?"
SPEAKER_02: "Sarah?"
SPEAKER_00: "That it wasn't true."
SPEAKER_00: "It wasn't real memory."
SPEAKER_02: "Yes."
SPEAKER_02: "Good."
SPEAKER_02: "Here's a distinction one critic raised."
SPEAKER_02: "This researcher, a leading expert in the field, said that the baby kicking after seeing the mobile is an example of implicit memory."
SPEAKER_02: "Implicit memory refers to remembering information, but not being consciously aware of remembering."
SPEAKER_02: "For example, many motor skills."

SPEAKER_02: "Remember those are skills involving movement of the body, that kind of thing?"
SPEAKER_02: "Motor skills are forms of implicit memory, like knowing how to ride a bike."
SPEAKER_02: "So the baby's kicking has to do with implicit memory because it involves perception and motor skills."
SPEAKER_02: "The baby kicks and sees a mobile move."
SPEAKER_02: "Now, explicit memory, on the other hand, is... Well, you tell me."
SPEAKER_02: "What is it?"
SPEAKER_00: "That's where you can remember something."
SPEAKER_00: "Remember it consciously."
SPEAKER_00: "You can remember what happened in the past."
SPEAKER_00: "That's what's usually meant by the word memory."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, real memory."
SPEAKER_02: "Okay."
SPEAKER_02: "And most researchers, they would say that ability in the area of explicit memory doesn't happen until a baby is six months to a year old."
SPEAKER_02: "Later than what Roe v. Collier said."
SPEAKER_02: "So that distinction between two different kinds of memory is important in this debate about memory and infants."

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_00: "Yesterday during our discussion of realism in art, I made reference to some new art forms and we'll be talking about several of these over the next few days, prints, watercolors, landscapes."
SPEAKER_00: "But I want to start with one you're probably less familiar with, panoramas."
SPEAKER_00: "Panoramas were huge canvases, sometimes several stories tall, and they were intended to be viewed in a circle."
SPEAKER_00: "In other words, they surrounded the viewer."
SPEAKER_00: "Incidentally, the word panorama, which is part of our everyday language now, was actually invented by a friend of Robert Barker, who was the first successful painter of this new art form."
SPEAKER_00: "Marker painted the first official panorama in 1787."
SPEAKER_00: "His accomplishment was remarkable for two reasons."
SPEAKER_00: "First, he figured out how to paint a circular painting so that it looked realistic."
SPEAKER_00: "Without going into all the details, Barker found a solution for the complicated perspective problems that this kind of painting presented."
SPEAKER_00: "The other key thing is that Barker recognized the need for a special viewing space to display this new kind of a painting."
SPEAKER_00: "In fact, a new kind of building."
SPEAKER_00: "Other people at that time were coming up with similar ideas, but Barker was the one who had the foresight to get a patent for it, which meant he had the right to the invention."

SPEAKER_00: "He described it as an apparatus for displaying views of nature at large."
SPEAKER_00: "The patent description said that the building was to be circular and it was only to allow light in from above."
SPEAKER_00: "It even specified the height of the platforms people would stand on and how far away they should be from the painting."
SPEAKER_00: "When Barker opened his new panorama building in London in 1794, it was a big hit."
SPEAKER_00: "But he didn't have a monopoly on his invention for long, as his license expired in 1801."
SPEAKER_00: "And in fact, after 1800, panoramas multiplied rapidly."
SPEAKER_00: "Practically every major city in Europe and North America had special buildings for displaying them."
SPEAKER_00: "Most of the buildings and paintings have disappeared, so we don't talk about panoramas much anymore."
SPEAKER_00: "But they were a very popular form of entertainment in their day, which doesn't mean that they weren't good art."
SPEAKER_00: "Many panoramas were painted under the supervision of first-rate artists who were aiming for a faithful reproduction of an actual scene."
SPEAKER_00: "Panoramic artists developed a number of techniques to intensify the illusion of reality for viewers, like special lighting effects."
SPEAKER_00: "And they would hide the edges of the painting with curtains, so there was no appearance of a frame or border at the top or bottom."

SPEAKER_00: "Sometimes they placed plants and trees in the space between the viewer and the painting."
SPEAKER_00: "In that respect, you see, panoramas were very much part of this new movement of realism."
SPEAKER_00: "Of course, paintings of large vistas had been done before."
SPEAKER_00: "It was a common subject for Renaissance painters."
SPEAKER_00: "But panorama painters transformed them by offering a full 360-degree view."
SPEAKER_00: "and the choice of perspective using a bird's eye view and an aerial perspective was new."
SPEAKER_00: "It's probably not a coincidence that hot air balloons made their debut around that time, or that the first high-rise buildings were going up."
SPEAKER_00: "As one writer put it, people were developing a panoramic consciousness."
SPEAKER_00: "Interestingly, a popular subject for panoramas was detailed views of the cities in which they were exhibited."

SPEAKER_00: "Why?"

SPEAKER_00: "Now, at this point, this active layer is saturated with water and heavier, allowing gravity to do its thing."
SPEAKER_00: "Below the active layer, the ground is still frozen, which is a perfect slide to move on."
SPEAKER_00: "Now in Spitzbergen, I'm sorry, but I hope you're not tired of hearing about the Arctic in Norway, but it's my area of interest, as you know."

SPEAKER_00: "It's a perfect visual example of this slow process."
SPEAKER_00: "In the Spitsbergen area, there are varying rates of solar flexion."
SPEAKER_00: "In some areas, it's slower than in others."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, why would that be?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, as you know, mass wasting is primarily a function of slope and gravity aided by water, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we can add one more factor."

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_01: "So, those are some of the common theories on bird migration."
SPEAKER_01: "Now let's talk about homing."
SPEAKER_01: "Homing is more than just the return flight home from migratory trips."
SPEAKER_01: "Homing can take place at any time and from any location."
SPEAKER_01: "It's the ability that some birds have of finding their way back to an exact location, regardless of their starting point, or how far away they are, or the time of year, and so on."
SPEAKER_01: "You've all heard of homing pigeons, but there are lots of bird species that have highly refined homing skills."
SPEAKER_01: "So maybe there's some evolutionary advantage."
SPEAKER_01: "Why would birds be so good at this?"
SPEAKER_00: "Todd?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, baby birds stay in the nest while the parents go out and hunt for food."
SPEAKER_00: "If the parents have to fly all over the place to find the food, they'd need to be able to find the nest again."
SPEAKER_01: "And once you've found food, you'd want to get home quickly, wouldn't you, to feed your young?"
SPEAKER_01: "So you'd want the most direct path home."
SPEAKER_01: "You wouldn't want to retrace whatever winding meandering path you'd taken while you were searching for food."
SPEAKER_01: "Being able to fly home directly is very efficient."
SPEAKER_01: "The offspring get food faster, and their parents are home sooner to look out for them."

SPEAKER_00: "But it isn't just the offspring that benefit."
SPEAKER_00: "The adult birds get back to a safe place sooner as well."
SPEAKER_01: "Right."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, what about finding your way home after going south for the winter?"
SPEAKER_02: "Well, it'd be nice if you could go back to a place you knew was okay."

SPEAKER_01: "I think so."

SPEAKER_02: "I mean, we talked about using the sun and the stars as navigational guides, and that would certainly be helpful for homing."
SPEAKER_02: "And so would that ability to sense Earth's magnetic poles, the one in the studies you described about the internal magnetic compass birds have."
SPEAKER_02: "Yeah, it should be the same."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, so celestial bodies make good navigational aids."
SPEAKER_01: "Anything else?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, for short distances you could just memorize the area, use landmarks, like when my friend tells me to turn left after I see the post office to get to his house."
SPEAKER_01: "So, when birds are out searching for food, they can memorize landmarks."
SPEAKER_01: "Mountains, rivers, who knows, maybe even the post office."
SPEAKER_01: "Studies of Gannets have shown that this is one way they navigate."
SPEAKER_01: "Gannets are seabirds, so when they wind up in an unfamiliar location over land, they fly in outward spirals until they reach the coast."
SPEAKER_01: "Then they use the coastline to find their way home."
SPEAKER_01: "Of course, when we say that birds memorize their terrain, that may not be accurate."
SPEAKER_01: "It's not always a good idea to use human analogies when trying to understand animal behavior, particularly when it comes to navigation, which we as a species are spectacularly bad at."
SPEAKER_01: "I mean, even with maps, how often do we get lost?"
SPEAKER_01: "But with birds, they're actually over-engineered to be good at navigation."

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_00: "Before the break, David asked a question."
SPEAKER_00: "When planning to start a business or even just to compete more effectively, how important is it to analyze your competitors?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, it's extremely important."
SPEAKER_00: "In particular, you need to try to understand the assumptions that the competition makes before you develop your own business strategy."
SPEAKER_00: "You see, every company makes assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, they may believe they've got a terrific sales force when maybe they really don't."
SPEAKER_00: "Or perhaps they think they've got the most innovative technology that's available when in fact there's something much more advanced out there."
SPEAKER_00: "But how do you go about identifying a competitor's assumptions?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there are a number of factors you could decide to look at, but I'd like to focus on two really important ones that, in my opinion, are quite frequently ignored."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, you need to look at your competitor's past, its history."
SPEAKER_00: "Companies are like people."

SPEAKER_00: "They respond to events, current events, based on their past experience, whether positive or negative."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, if a company has always been successful against its competitors, it may assume that it can't or won't ever lose those battles."
SPEAKER_00: "Or, and this is a lot more common, if it's had a significant failure in its past, it may assume that it should never again try to engage in that particular activity."
SPEAKER_00: "Clearly a smart company learns from its mistakes."
SPEAKER_00: "But companies aren't always good at recognizing when the business environments changed."
SPEAKER_00: "They don't always realize when past experience is no longer a good predictor of future performance."
SPEAKER_00: "What else?"
SPEAKER_00: "Oh yeah, you also need to take an in-depth look at the leadership of the company."
SPEAKER_00: "Even the biggest corporations are run by some group of individuals, and their personal experiences will play a large role in establishing the company's assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "Managers have histories too."
SPEAKER_00: "They're influenced by past successes and failures just the way corporations are."

SPEAKER_00: "Those personal histories shape their management assumptions, but it's not just the past experiences they've had at that particular company that influence managers."
SPEAKER_00: "Some managers are hired from other companies rather than being promoted from within, and these outsiders bring new perspectives which may not fit neatly with the company's existing assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "We see this a lot, especially if the new manager is not coming in with the experiences from the same business sector, but rather from one that operates with very different rules and assumptions, like moving from making automobiles to selling insurance."
SPEAKER_00: "There's another reason to study the leadership."
SPEAKER_00: "We expect top executives to be generalists."
SPEAKER_00: "They have to be familiar with all aspects of their businesses, but the reality is that most of them have some specific area of expertise that influences their decision making."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, an executive with a financial background can't help viewing the world differently from a manager whose training is mostly in production."
SPEAKER_00: "But you can make some pretty good guesses about how their backgrounds may influence their strategic assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "If the top person selects a management team where everyone has the same functional background, well, how hard is it going to be to determine that team's assumptions?"
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, let's imagine that we've made a comprehensive list of a competitor's assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "Now we need to figure out if those assumptions are accurate."

SPEAKER_00: "I'd like to talk today about about an important paradigm in the history of the United States and how it came to be changed upon a second look in 1893."
SPEAKER_00: "An eminent American historian by the name of Frederick Jackson Turner delivered a lecture at the American Historical Association and his lecture was entitled the significance of the frontier in American history."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, when he was talking about the frontier, he was referring to the part of the American West that wasn't yet settled."
SPEAKER_00: "The point Turner made in his lecture was that the 1890 US Census had provided new data about settlement and those data showed that well, there was no longer a frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "This disappearance of the frontier seemed to him tremendously significant and prompted him to gather ideas about how the frontier might have shaped American history and the American character."
SPEAKER_00: "Turner asserted that the existence of a frontier with cheaper or virtually free land for the taking available for individuals to sort of try their luck tended to promote certain ideals that were central to the American character."
SPEAKER_00: "ideals of economic self-sufficiency, individualism, political independence, small town democracy, suspicion of large government, things like that."
SPEAKER_00: "He saw these things being really shaped by the economic and social conditions of the frontier."

SPEAKER_00: "And for Turner, the end of the frontier in 1890 meant the end of this particular era of American character and that Americans would become less individualistic, less independent minded."
SPEAKER_00: "And the frontier thesis was pretty much the accepted paradigm of the history of the American West until the 1970s."
SPEAKER_00: "Then, for a variety of reasons, historians studying the American West began to question this thesis, and ultimately, many of them ended up pretty much turning it on its head."
SPEAKER_00: "And this is what fascinates me about this debate."
SPEAKER_00: "The change that happened was not so much a matter of people finding new evidence that hadn't been available to Turner."
SPEAKER_00: "It was more a question of people looking at the same evidence with a different eye, with different emphases."
SPEAKER_00: "There are two main areas of challenge to Turner that arose in what's been called the New Western history."
SPEAKER_00: "One was that Turner tended to overemphasize the importance of Americans of European descent on the Western frontier."

SPEAKER_00: "This was somewhat of an unconscious bias on his part, but you know, if you looked at Turner's picture of the frontier, it was basically white men from Europe who set the tone and defined the culture."
SPEAKER_00: "New Western historians took another look at the West and realized that it was actually a pretty culturally diverse place."
SPEAKER_00: "You had obviously American Indians, Mexicans, Chinese and others."
SPEAKER_00: "So the cultural uniformity of the frontier that Turner saw, the New Western historians look at it and see something very, very different."
SPEAKER_00: "The other main area of challenge by the New Western historians was that Turner really, in their view, underestimated the role of the federal government and large corporations in shaping the economic life of the frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, just as a, for instance, the federal government to this day is the largest landowner west of the Mississippi River."
SPEAKER_00: "And people's lives when they got to the frontier were really dictated by market forces beyond, pretty much beyond their control."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, you grew wheat, you exported it to Europe, and whether you prospered or not depended at least partly on the price of wheat."

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_00: "I'd like to talk today about about an important paradigm in the history of the United States and how it came to be changed upon a second look in 1893."
SPEAKER_00: "An eminent American historian by the name of Frederick Jackson Turner delivered a lecture at the American Historical Association and his lecture was entitled the significance of the frontier in American history."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, when he was talking about the frontier, he was referring to the part of the American West that wasn't yet settled."
SPEAKER_00: "The point Turner made in his lecture was that the 1890 US Census had provided new data about settlement and those data showed that well, there was no longer a frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "This disappearance of the frontier seemed to him tremendously significant and prompted him to gather ideas about how the frontier might have shaped American history and the American character."
SPEAKER_00: "Turner asserted that the existence of a frontier with cheaper or virtually free land for the taking available for individuals to sort of try their luck tended to promote certain ideals that were central to the American character."
SPEAKER_00: "ideals of economic self-sufficiency, individualism, political independence, small town democracy, suspicion of large government, things like that."
SPEAKER_00: "He saw these things being really shaped by the economic and social conditions of the frontier."

SPEAKER_00: "And for Turner, the end of the frontier in 1890 meant the end of this particular era of American character and that Americans would become less individualistic, less independent minded."
SPEAKER_00: "And the frontier thesis was pretty much the accepted paradigm of the history of the American West until the 1970s."
SPEAKER_00: "Then, for a variety of reasons, historians studying the American West began to question this thesis, and ultimately, many of them ended up pretty much turning it on its head."
SPEAKER_00: "And this is what fascinates me about this debate."
SPEAKER_00: "The change that happened was not so much a matter of people finding new evidence that hadn't been available to Turner."
SPEAKER_00: "It was more a question of people looking at the same evidence with a different eye, with different emphases."
SPEAKER_00: "There are two main areas of challenge to Turner that arose in what's been called the New Western history."
SPEAKER_00: "One was that Turner tended to overemphasize the importance of Americans of European descent on the Western frontier."

SPEAKER_00: "This was somewhat of an unconscious bias on his part, but you know, if you looked at Turner's picture of the frontier, it was basically white men from Europe who set the tone and defined the culture."
SPEAKER_00: "New Western historians took another look at the West and realized that it was actually a pretty culturally diverse place."
SPEAKER_00: "You had obviously American Indians, Mexicans, Chinese and others."
SPEAKER_00: "So the cultural uniformity of the frontier that Turner saw, the New Western historians look at it and see something very, very different."
SPEAKER_00: "The other main area of challenge by the New Western historians was that Turner really, in their view, underestimated the role of the federal government and large corporations in shaping the economic life of the frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, just as a, for instance, the federal government to this day is the largest landowner west of the Mississippi River."
SPEAKER_00: "And people's lives when they got to the frontier were really dictated by market forces beyond, pretty much beyond their control."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, you grew wheat, you exported it to Europe, and whether you prospered or not depended at least partly on the price of wheat."

SPEAKER_00: "He laid his wall-size canvas flat on the floor so he could move around it and work it from all sides."

SPEAKER_00: "Now the physical movements involved in Pollock's painting technique have led people to call it action painting, which almost suggests that the process of creating the painting physically was at least as important as the end product itself."
SPEAKER_00: "In fact, people used to watch him work in his studio, dripping and pouring paint and other materials onto his canvases."
SPEAKER_00: "This could make you think of Pollock's work as being kind of like wild or chaotic or random."

SPEAKER_00: "Pollock used massive canvases."

SPEAKER_00: "A good example of his technique is the painting Autumn Rhythm, which Pollock painted in 1950."
SPEAKER_00: "Autumn Rhythm, at first glance, looks like basically just a whole lot of squiggly lines."
SPEAKER_00: "Rather bizarre, just like a bunch of pointless drips and swirls."
SPEAKER_00: "But if you look closely, you see why it's so admired."
SPEAKER_00: "Beneath all the apparent chaos, there's really a very definite structure of lines, rhythms and sensations that makes the whole piece work."
